antiPatterns.title: Missing legend
antiPatterns.body: The trailing paragraph naming filled/half/empty is what onboards a first-time reader. Skipping it forces the audience to guess the mapping.

related.name: compare-table
related.when: cells are textual values, not state markers

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: options scored against criteria with a per-card layout instead of a table

related.name: matrix-2x2
related.when: two axes, four cells, qualitative placement

related.name: checklist
related.when: one set of obligations against one regime, not many

components.name: piechart
components.description: Pie or donut chart with legend — proportional wedges.
components.purpose: Use for part-to-whole breakdowns with three to six slices. Add the `donut` modifier for a hole in the middle — visually cleaner for executive decks.

slices.description: One li per slice: label text then a trailing inline-code value pill, e.g. - Marketing `40%` (slices are drawn proportionally to the values).

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: piechart donut -->

`Eyebrow · context`

## What the breakdown shows.

- First slice `40%`
- Second slice `30%`
- Third slice `20%`
- Fourth slice `10%`

whenToUse.title: Three to six parts of a whole
whenToUse.body: Time allocation, budget breakdown, mix-of-business. Past six slices the wedges become unreadable and the legend overwhelms — split or pick the top five plus an `Other` slice.

whenToUse.title: Proportions matter more than precision
whenToUse.body: Pie charts are good at 'roughly a third', bad at 'is it 28% or 31%?'. If exact differences are the argument, reach for a bar chart (`progress`) where the eye can compare lengths directly.

whenToUse.title: Donut for executive decks
whenToUse.body: The `donut` modifier hollows the centre — cleaner, less crowded, and the hole reads as composed rather than as a missing slice. Default to donut for board / investor decks; reserve solid pies for analyst working sessions.

antiPatterns.title: Slices that don't sum to a whole
antiPatterns.body: A pie of unrelated metrics is meaningless — the visual implies parts of a whole. If your values are independent measures, use stats or a bar chart instead.

antiPatterns.title: Two slices
antiPatterns.body: A two-slice pie is just a percentage with extra steps. Use big-number or split-metric — the audience can read '38% / 62%' faster than they can decode a half-and-half disc.

antiPatterns.title: Comparing two pies
antiPatterns.body: Side-by-side pies force the audience to compare wedge angles across two figures — humans are bad at this. Use grouped bars or a slope chart to land the comparison cleanly.

related.name: progress
related.when: comparable parts but precise differences matter

related.name: stats
related.when: the values are independent metrics, not a partition

related.name: big-number
related.when: the headline is one slice, not the breakdown

related.name: kpi
related.when: the slices need status framing and targets

components.name: progress
components.description: Horizontal progress bars — one row per item, percentage filled.
components.purpose: Use for status-tracking across multiple parallel items (project readiness, OKR progress, capacity utilization). Status colors via on-track/at-risk/blocked.

title.description: Slide heading framing the progress view.

eyebrow.description: Optional eyebrow caption above the heading.

subtitle.description: Optional plain subtitle after the heading.

rows.description: One li per item: label text then trailing inline-code pills — percent first, optional status second, e.g. - Adoption `68%` `at-risk`. Status vocabulary: on-track / live / at-risk / warn / blocked / fail / deferred / done. An optional nested bullet renders as a per-row note.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: progress -->

`Eyebrow · context`

## Progress by item.

- First item `80%` `on-track`
- Second item `55%` `at-risk`
- Third item `30%` `blocked`

whenToUse.title: Parallel workstreams at a glance
whenToUse.body: When the audience needs to scan five to eight workstreams and immediately spot the ones in trouble. The bar length carries the magnitude; the status pill carries the verdict.

whenToUse.title: Percent-complete is the natural unit
whenToUse.body: Readiness, OKR progress, capacity utilization, rollout coverage. Any series where each row is a 0–100% completion against its own scale fits the layout. Mixed units belong in `kpi`.

whenToUse.title: Status framing matters as much as the number
whenToUse.body: Use the `on-track`, `at-risk`, `blocked`, `deferred`, `done` vocabulary — the engine tints the bar fill to match. A 68% bar reads very differently when it is `at-risk` than when it is `on-track`.

antiPatterns.title: Comparing unrelated metrics
antiPatterns.body: Revenue % of target, latency vs SLO, and headcount fill aren't comparable on a shared bar scale. Use `kpi` for value/target/status tiles or `stats` for an independent metric row.

antiPatterns.title: More than eight rows
antiPatterns.body: Past eight workstreams the bars compress and the labels truncate. Split the view by owner or workstream group; the audience can't scan twelve bars at once anyway.

antiPatterns.title: Decorative status pills
antiPatterns.body: Don't invent new status words for tone. `on-track`, `at-risk`, `blocked`, `deferred`, `done` are the vocabulary the engine recognises; everything else renders as a plain pill and breaks the at-a-glance read.

related.name: kpi
related.when: value + target + status tiles, not a single percent

related.name: stats
related.when: independent headline metrics, no completion scale

related.name: gantt
related.when: the rows are time-bound and need a date axis

related.name: checklist
related.when: binary done / not-done across a flat list

related.name: timeline-list
related.when: the workstreams complete in sequence, not in parallel

components.name: quadrant
components.description: Native 2×2 scatter chart — items plotted on two continuous axes.
components.purpose: Use to position items by two numeric attributes (cost × value, effort × impact). Data-driven; for static categorical 2×2 grids, use matrix-2x2.

items.description: One li per item. Format: `Label — x, y[, size]`.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: quadrant -->

`Effort 0–10 → Reach 0–100`

## Where to put the next dollar, having spent the last one on a workshop.

Effort estimated in story-points; reach as percent of addressable teams.

- Strategic Bets
  - Scoring model v2 `3, 70`
  - Per-team calibration `5, 85`
- Quick Wins
  - Weekly signal brief `8, 80`
  - Snapshot exports `9, 55`
- Defer
  - Vendor scoping `2, 30`
  - Manual recalibration `1, 22`
- Time Sinks
  - Custom audit log UI `7, 18`
  - Bespoke board export `9, 28`

whenToUse.title: Two numeric axes carry the analysis
whenToUse.body: Effort × impact, cost × value, probability × severity, reach × confidence. Both axes are continuous and the position on each genuinely matters — that's the argument quadrant is built to make.

whenToUse.title: Categorical grouping clusters the dots
whenToUse.body: Items grouped under list headings (`Strategic Bets`, `Quick Wins`, `Defer`, `Time Sinks`) share a colour, so the eye can read the cluster before the individual point. The grouping is editorial, not derived from coordinates.

whenToUse.title: Six to twelve items
whenToUse.body: Below six the chart wastes the canvas — write it as prose. Past twelve the labels overlap and the quadrant becomes a constellation. Trim the long tail or break it across two slides.

antiPatterns.title: Static categorical 2×2
antiPatterns.body: If the quadrants are fixed labels (Important × Urgent, Build × Buy × Partner × Defer) and items are placed by category not coordinate, use `matrix-2x2`. `quadrant` is data-driven; `matrix-2x2` is conceptual.

antiPatterns.title: Single axis matters
antiPatterns.body: If one axis is decorative and only the other carries meaning, you have a ranking, not a scatter. Use `progress` for percent-complete or `kpi` for ranked metrics with status.

antiPatterns.title: Coordinates without an audience-shared scale
antiPatterns.body: If `8, 80` requires a footnote to interpret, the slide doesn't pay off. Either label the axis units in the eyebrow (`Effort 0–10 → Reach 0–100`) or normalise to a familiar scale before authoring.

related.name: matrix-2x2
related.when: the 2×2 is categorical, not coordinate-based

related.name: radar
related.when: items rated across more than two criteria

related.name: progress
related.when: percent-complete on a single axis

related.name: piechart
related.when: part-to-whole, not bivariate position

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: comparing options against shared categorical criteria

components.name: quote
components.description: A pulled quotation, centered, with attribution.
components.purpose: Use to land a phrase verbatim — customer voice, expert claim, mission statement. Keep under ~25 words. The quote IS the slide; the attribution is the supporting credit.
components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: quote -->

> The quoted sentence sits here, kept short enough to read in one breath.

Attribution — Person, Role

whenToUse.title: Verbatim language matters
whenToUse.body: When the audience needs to hear the words exactly as they were said — customer feedback, expert claim, regulatory text, mission statement. Paraphrasing would lose the impact.

whenToUse.title: One breath of reading
whenToUse.body: Keep the quotation under ~25 words. The audience reads silently in one breath; longer than that and they're scanning, not feeling the words.

whenToUse.title: Attribution earns the quote
whenToUse.body: Anonymous quotes feel weak. Attribute the speaker, their role, and the context ("Head of Product, Pilot Team 3" beats just "a customer"). The attribution is the credibility.

antiPatterns.title: Paragraph-length quotes
antiPatterns.body: If the quote runs past 25 words, the slide is reading like a wall of text. Trim aggressively or use `split-statement` (gives the quote half the slide alongside spelled-out implications).

antiPatterns.title: Multiple quotes per slide
antiPatterns.body: Two quotes on one canvas dilute both. The whole point is that one quote earns the whole slide. For a montage of customer voices, use successive quote slides.

antiPatterns.title: Decorative quotes
antiPatterns.body: If the quote could be paraphrased without losing anything, the slide doesn't need to be a quote slide. Move the idea into `content` and skip the chrome.

related.name: split-statement
related.when: the quote needs implications spelled out alongside it

related.name: content
related.when: the language is paraphrasable — let prose carry it

related.name: big-number
related.when: the most memorable thing is a metric, not a phrase

components.name: radar
components.description: Native radar / spider chart — items rated across multiple axes.
components.purpose: Use to compare 2–4 options across the same 4–8 criteria. Each option becomes a polygon; overlap shows where strengths align.

series.description: One li per series (option). Format: `Label — v1, v2, v3, v4, …` one number per axis.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: radar -->

`Scale · 0–10`

## How we stack up across the buying criteria.

- Lattice
  - Performance `9`
  - Pricing `7`
  - Support `8`
  - Ecosystem `6`
  - Security `9`
- Rival North
  - Performance `7`
  - Pricing `8`
  - Support `6`
  - Ecosystem `9`
  - Security `7`
- Rival West
  - Performance `6`
  - Pricing `9`
  - Support `7`
  - Ecosystem `8`
  - Security `8`

whenToUse.title: Same criteria, multiple options
whenToUse.body: Competitive comparison, vendor evaluation, candidate scoring — anywhere two to four options need to be rated on the same four-to-eight criteria. The polygon shapes show the trade-off pattern at a glance.

whenToUse.title: Shape is the argument
whenToUse.body: Radar charts are good at 'we are strong here and weak there' — the lopsided polygon is the message. If precise pairwise comparisons matter more than the silhouette, use a grouped bar chart or `verdict-grid`.

whenToUse.title: Shared zero-to-ten scale
whenToUse.body: Every axis must use the same scale so the polygons are comparable. Mixed units (ms, $, count) collapse the chart's meaning — normalise to a shared 0–10 or 0–100 before authoring.

antiPatterns.title: More than four series
antiPatterns.body: Five overlapping polygons become a tangle of edges. Trim to the two or three options the audience is actually choosing between; the rest belong in an appendix table.

antiPatterns.title: Three or fewer axes
antiPatterns.body: Three axes makes a triangle — barely a shape. Below four criteria, the spider collapses and the slide should be a `cards-grid` or `verdict-grid` instead.

antiPatterns.title: Mixed scales across axes
antiPatterns.body: If one axis is 0–10 and another is 0–10,000, the larger axis dominates the polygon and the comparison is misleading. Normalise everything to a shared scale first.

related.name: quadrant
related.when: two axes are enough — the other six dimensions drop out

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: the criteria are categorical (pass/fail), not graded

related.name: kpi
related.when: the comparison is one option's metrics, not multi-option

related.name: compare-table
related.when: a precise tabular comparison reads better than a shape

related.name: piechart
related.when: the question is part-to-whole, not multi-criterion

components.name: redline
components.description: Clause-by-clause comparison — verbatim language with inline <ins>/<del> tracking the amendment.
components.purpose: Use when an amendment's diff is the slide. The blockquote carries the redlined text with ins/del markers; the trailing list explains why the diff matters operationally.

heading.description: Slide heading naming the amendment or change.

citation.description: Inline-code citation of the amended provision (e.g. 'Cal. Civ. Code §1798.135 · SB-362 (2024)').

redline.description: The amended language. Use <del>old text</del> and <ins>new text</ins> inline.

implications.description: Optional explanation. Use **Why this matters** for the operational read.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: redline -->

## Headline naming the amendment.

`Citation reference · amendment name (year)`

> Verbatim language with <del>old wording</del> <ins>new wording</ins> inline so the diff reads cleanly.

- **Why this matters.** What the amendment changes in operational terms, in one sentence.

whenToUse.title: Verbatim text matters
whenToUse.body: When the amendment is the language — legal clauses, regulatory paragraphs, contract terms. Paraphrasing would lose the exact words the parties are bound by.

whenToUse.title: Diff is the slide
whenToUse.body: The inline `<ins>` and `<del>` markers are the editorial argument. The audience reads what changed by scanning the green and red inline marks, not by toggling between two blocks.

whenToUse.title: Why-this-matters trailer
whenToUse.body: End with a single operational sentence explaining what the diff means for the team's work. The clause is the evidence; the trailer is the implication.

antiPatterns.title: Code diffs
antiPatterns.body: For two code snippets side-by-side, use `compare-code`. redline is for natural language — legal, regulatory, contractual — not source code.

antiPatterns.title: Paraphrased clauses
antiPatterns.body: If the quoted text isn't verbatim, the diff is meaningless. Either render the actual clause with its diff, or move to `compare-prose` for narrative comparison.

antiPatterns.title: Long passage with one tiny diff
antiPatterns.body: If one word changed in a paragraph, quote only the affected sentence or two. A wall of unchanged text with one inline mark buries the change.

related.name: compare-code
related.when: the diff is source code, not natural language

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: two narrative alternatives, not verbatim amendments

related.name: before-after
related.when: the change is structural state, not text

related.name: obligation-matrix
related.when: comparing many regimes against shared obligations

components.name: regulatory-update
components.description: Change log against a baseline — numbered list of statutes/cases/rules with citation, summary, and effective date.
components.purpose: Use when a quarter's regulatory motion needs a single-slide digest. Each row carries the change name, the citation (inline code), the summary, and the effective-date marker (inline code).

heading.description: Slide heading framing the period or theme of the changes.

scope.description: Optional inline-code scope label (e.g. 'Federal · State · International').

items.description: Ordered list of changes. Each item leads with a plain text name; nested ul carries citation (code), summary, and effective date (code).

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: regulatory-update -->

## Headline naming the period or theme.

`Scope label · jurisdiction tier`

1. Change name
   - `Citation reference`
   - Summary in one sentence.
   - `Effective Mon YYYY`
2. Change name
   - `Citation reference`
   - Summary in one sentence.
   - `Effective Mon YYYY`
3. Change name
   - `Citation reference`
   - Summary in one sentence.
   - `Effective Mon YYYY`

whenToUse.title: Period-bounded digest
whenToUse.body: When a quarter or half of regulatory motion needs to land as a single scannable ledger. The audience sees what moved, when it took effect, and where to read it — without flipping through a multi-page memo.

whenToUse.title: Citation is the proof
whenToUse.body: Each row anchors on its inline-code citation chip. Without the citation the row reads as opinion; with it, the row earns its place in a compliance brief.

whenToUse.title: Effective-date pill closes the row
whenToUse.body: The trailing `Effective Mon YYYY` chip tells the audience whether the change is live, imminent, or final. The pill is the row's call-to-action signal.

antiPatterns.title: Single rule's lineage
antiPatterns.body: If the slide walks one rule from statute through case, use `authority-chain`. regulatory-update is a period digest.

antiPatterns.title: Past six rows
antiPatterns.body: More than six items compresses the row gap and the citation chips run out of room. Split by jurisdiction.

antiPatterns.title: Missing summary or citation
antiPatterns.body: Each row needs all three sub-items — citation, summary, effective date. Otherwise the row reads as rumour.

related.name: authority-chain
related.when: single rule walked statute → regulation → guidance → case

related.name: list-criteria
related.when: flat enumeration of requirements without dates or citations

related.name: timeline
related.when: chronological sequence of events on a single axis

related.name: list-tabular
related.when: structured metadata per row but no regulatory framing

components.name: roadmap
components.description: Phased multi-workstream grid — phases across the top, workstreams down the side.
components.purpose: Use to show what ships in each phase across multiple parallel workstreams. Cells render as state-token discs (pass/warn/fail/skip).

rows.description: A markdown table. The header row lists the phases (each may carry an inline-code date pill, e.g. `Q2 2026`); the first column is the workstream name; each cell leads with a state marker [x]/[-]/[ ]/[/] then the deliverable.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: roadmap -->

`H2 2026 · Plan`

## What ships in each phase, by workstream.

| Workstream | Foundation `Q2 2026` | Hardening `Q3 2026` | Scale `Q4 2026` |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| First workstream | [x] Shipped item | [-] In-flight item | [ ] Planned item |
| Second workstream | [x] Shipped item | [/] Out-of-scope item | [ ] Planned item |

State markers `[x]/[-]/[ ]/[/]` are universal: ✓ shipped, ◐ in flight, ○ planned, ╱ out of scope.

whenToUse.title: Phased delivery across workstreams
whenToUse.body: When the question is what each team ships in each phase. Workstreams down the side, phases across the top, deliverables in the cells — the whole plan reads in one glance.

whenToUse.title: State markers are the second channel
whenToUse.body: Every cell can lead with `[x]` shipped, `[-]` in flight, `[ ]` planned, or `[/]` out of scope. The audience sees both 'what' and 'how it's going' without a separate status slide.

whenToUse.title: Phase headers carry meta pills
whenToUse.body: Append `` `Q2 2026` `` to a phase header and the renderer anchors a meta pill on the right of the column. Use it for date, owner, or status tags that frame the phase.

antiPatterns.title: One workstream
antiPatterns.body: A single row of phases is a `timeline` or `list-steps`, not a roadmap. Roadmap earns its grid only when at least two workstreams move in parallel.

antiPatterns.title: No state markers
antiPatterns.body: A grid of bare deliverables loses half its value. Add `[x]`/`[-]`/`[ ]`/`[/]` so the audience reads progress alongside scope.

antiPatterns.title: Past five workstreams
antiPatterns.body: More than five rows compresses cell text and the lane stripes lose their categorical read. Group adjacent workstreams or split by phase.

related.name: gantt
related.when: continuous task bars across a date axis rather than discrete phase cells

related.name: kanban
related.when: current state by stage rather than phased schedule

related.name: list-steps
related.when: single workstream sequence without parallel lanes

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: options scored against shared criteria, not phased delivery

related.name: checklist
related.when: single list with state markers, no workstream dimension

components.name: split-brief
components.description: Executive brief — dark left anchor, findings list on the right with left-rule chrome.
components.purpose: Use when one paragraph of executive context needs to read alongside three or four substantiating findings. The dark left panel is the anchor; the right panel is the evidence.

eyebrow.description: Optional inline-code eyebrow above the heading.

heading.description: Heading shown in the dark left anchor.

lede.description: One-sentence framing paragraph under the heading.

findings.description: Right-side findings. Each li's lead is the finding title — it renders bold automatically (no `**…**` needed); follow it with a nested `- body` line. A bare single-line bullet won't render bold.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: split-brief -->

`Eyebrow context`

## Headline that anchors the brief.

One-sentence framing paragraph explaining what the findings cover.

- First finding
  - Supporting detail explaining the first finding.
- Second finding
  - Supporting detail explaining the second finding.
- Third finding
  - Supporting detail explaining the third finding.

whenToUse.title: Executive context + findings
whenToUse.body: When the slide needs both a paragraph of framing AND a list of substantiating points. The dark left panel carries the framing; the right list carries the evidence. Pure list (no framing) belongs in `list` or `cards-stack`.

whenToUse.title: Three to four findings
whenToUse.body: Sweet spot is 3-4 findings. Fewer wastes the layout's grid; more crowds the right panel and the audience loses scannability.

whenToUse.title: Executive audiences
whenToUse.body: The brief shape mirrors how analysts and execs structure memos: thesis on the left, evidence on the right. Reach for split-brief when the deck reads like a memo, not a presentation.

antiPatterns.title: More than 5 findings
antiPatterns.body: Past 5 findings, the right panel becomes a wall of bullets and the layout's balance breaks. Split into two slides or use `cards-stack` for a fuller list.

antiPatterns.title: Lede that's not a sentence
antiPatterns.body: The lede is the framing. A fragment or eyebrow-style phrase wastes the role. Write one declarative sentence that sets up why the findings matter.

antiPatterns.title: Findings without titles
antiPatterns.body: The title at the start of each li — rendered bold automatically — is what makes the right panel scannable. A naked sentence per bullet reads as paragraph soup; the bold lead is the structure.

related.name: split-list
related.when: the right side is a list of supporting points, not findings

related.name: split-statement
related.when: the left side carries a quote, not a thesis

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: the slide is mostly the list of findings; no executive framing needed

related.name: tldr
related.when: 5+ one-line takeaways without supporting detail

components.name: split-compare
components.description: Two options + verdict — dark frame on the left, 2-column option grid + a recommendation card on the right.
components.purpose: Use when a decision frames a binary choice and the recommendation must be unambiguous. Second top-level list item is always the preferred option (gets the accent badge). The verdict blockquote becomes a recommendation card with a corner tag, pinned across the bottom.

frame.description: Optional inline-code frame label above the heading (e.g. 'Decision Required').

heading.description: Decision framing in the dark left panel.

context.description: One-sentence context paragraph under the heading.

options.description: Exactly two top-level items. First is the alternative; second is the preferred option.

verdict.description: The recommendation — one short sentence in a blockquote.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: split-compare -->

`Decision Required`

## Headline that frames the choice.

One-sentence context paragraph explaining the stakes.

- Alternative option
  - First fact about the alternative
  - Second fact about the alternative
- Preferred option
  - First fact about the preferred path
  - Second fact about the preferred path

> The recommendation in one decisive sentence.

whenToUse.title: Binary decision with a recommendation
whenToUse.body: The slide must close the question with one chosen path. Use when the audience needs both the trade-off and the verdict on one slide, not on two.

whenToUse.title: Comparable facts per option
whenToUse.body: Each side carries 2–4 short bullets — the same kind of fact across both. Lopsided facts break the visual symmetry.

whenToUse.title: Verdict in one sentence
whenToUse.body: The trailing blockquote is the recommendation distilled to a single line. Anything longer belongs in spoken commentary, not the recommendation card.

antiPatterns.title: Three or more options
antiPatterns.body: split-compare is strictly two — first card is the alternative, second card is the preferred. For three options, use `verdict-grid` or successive `decision` slides.

antiPatterns.title: No verdict
antiPatterns.body: The blockquote is mandatory. Without it the slide collapses to a comparison without a call — use `compare-prose` for that case.

antiPatterns.title: Preferred on the left
antiPatterns.body: Layout convention pins the preferred option to the second (right) card. Putting the recommendation first breaks the reading flow and the accent badge lands on the wrong card.

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: the comparison is undecided — no verdict bar yet

related.name: decision
related.when: the verdict slide that follows a separate comparison

related.name: split-brief
related.when: the right side is findings, not paired options

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: three or more options scored against shared criteria

components.name: split-list
components.description: Featured left panel + supporting list on the right.
components.purpose: Use when one prominent statement deserves a dark sidebar and the right side carries the substantiating points.

panel-heading.description: Heading shown in the dark left panel.

panel-eyebrow.description: Optional rubric — a section label that renders in the RIGHT content panel above the points list (despite the slot name, it is not a left-panel eyebrow).

points.description: Right-side supporting points. The lead text of each li is the point label — it renders bold automatically (no `**…**` needed); follow it with a nested bullet for the body text.

meta.description: Optional metadata footer. A SECOND bullet list placed after the points list (separate the two with an HTML comment so markdown does not merge them). Its first two items pin to the bottom of the right panel with injected 'Audience ·' and 'Intent ·' labels.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: split-list -->

## Panel headline that frames the side points.

### Section rubric

- First point
  - Supporting sentence explaining the first point.
- Second point
  - Supporting sentence explaining the second point.
- Third point
  - Supporting sentence explaining the third point.

whenToUse.title: Thesis plus proof
whenToUse.body: One bold claim deserves dedicated visual weight on the left; supporting points sit to the right. Use when the audience needs to hear the claim before the evidence.

whenToUse.title: Section openers with substance
whenToUse.body: More substantive than a divider, but still anchored by a single statement. Three to four supporting points keep the panel from feeling cluttered.

whenToUse.title: Mirror for image-heavy decks
whenToUse.body: The `mirror` variant flips the panel to the right. Useful when the deck's visual rhythm wants accents on alternating sides.

antiPatterns.title: Longer than four points
antiPatterns.body: If the right side runs past four points, the panel and list lose balance. Move to `cards-stack` for longer lists or split into two slides.

antiPatterns.title: No panel headline
antiPatterns.body: The dark panel exists to carry a statement. An empty or generic heading wastes the strongest visual real estate on the slide.

antiPatterns.title: Stacking many split-list slides in a row
antiPatterns.body: The dark sidebar is a strong visual signal. Three split-list slides back-to-back numbs the audience to it. Intersperse with content or stats.

related.name: split-statement
related.when: the left panel carries a pull quote (not a section eyebrow + heading)

related.name: split-brief
related.when: dark left panel (heading + lede) + left-rule findings list on the right

related.name: big-number
related.when: single statement, no supporting list — the full-canvas version

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: the right-side list grows past four points

components.name: split-metric
components.description: One number owns the room — light left with the hero metric, dark right with supporting findings.
components.purpose: Use when a single KPI is the argument. The hero number is the headline; the right-side list explains why the number matters and what the trend implies.

unit.description: Optional inline-code unit label above the metric (e.g. 'Net Revenue Retention').

metric.description: The hero number. Wrap a unit in *italics* (e.g. '114*%*') to render at smaller weight.

context.description: One-sentence context line below the metric — measurement window, cohort, comparison.

findings.description: Right-side supporting findings. Each li's lead is the finding title — it renders bold automatically (no `**…**` needed); follow it with a nested `- body` line. A bare single-line bullet won't render bold.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: split-metric -->

`Metric name`

## 114*%*

Measurement window and qualifying detail in one short sentence.

- First supporting point.
  - Why this metric matters and what's driving it.
- Second supporting point.
  - What concentration or trend explains it.
- Third supporting point.
  - What this number unlocks or threatens.

whenToUse.title: One KPI deserves the room
whenToUse.body: When a single number — NRR, NPS, gross margin, error budget burn — carries the slide and the audience needs to see why it matters. The hero scale on the left earns the focus; the right panel earns the explanation.

whenToUse.title: Three findings, not three numbers
whenToUse.body: The right panel is for the implications of the headline number — the driver, the trend, the unlock. If the panel needs four more metrics to explain the first, use `kpi` instead.

whenToUse.title: Italics for the unit
whenToUse.body: Wrap the unit in `*…*` (e.g. `114*%*`, `4.2*×*`, `$420*M*`) so the headline reads as 'one-fourteen percent' rather than letting the symbol dominate the eye. The italic glyph drops a weight relative to the number.

antiPatterns.title: Two metrics on the left panel
antiPatterns.body: If two numbers compete for the hero slot the layout breaks visually. Either trust one number to carry the slide or move to `stats` for a metric row.

antiPatterns.title: Findings that just restate the number
antiPatterns.body: Bullets like 'NRR is 114%' or 'Up from 110%' don't earn the panel — the headline already said that. Use the right-side findings for the driver, the cohort, and the unlock.

antiPatterns.title: No context line under the metric
antiPatterns.body: A number with no measurement window or cohort is a boast, not a finding. The context line is what makes the metric audit-defensible — name the cohort, the window, the comparison.

related.name: big-number
related.when: the number is the whole slide — no supporting findings needed

related.name: stats
related.when: a row of comparable headline metrics instead of one focal KPI

related.name: kpi
related.when: the metric needs targets, status pills, and a dashboard frame

related.name: split-statement
related.when: the left is a quote, not a metric

related.name: split-brief
related.when: the left is a thesis statement, not a number

components.name: split-statement
components.description: Pull quote — half the slide committed to one quoted idea on a dark left panel, supporting list on the right.
components.purpose: Use when one quotation deserves the full attention of a slide and the implications need spelling out. Distinct from `quote` (which is the whole slide); split-statement gives equal room to quote and consequences.

quotation.description: The pull quote — one or two sentences, italic display font in the dark left panel.

cite.description: Optional attribution in an inline-code paragraph after the blockquote.

implications.description: Right-side supporting points. Each li's lead is the implication title — it renders bold automatically (no `**…**` needed); follow it with a nested `- body` line. A bare single-line bullet won't render bold.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: split-statement -->

> The quotation, one or two sentences worth committing half the slide to.

`Speaker · Role, Organisation, Year`

- First implication
  - What this quote means for the work in front of us.
- Second implication
  - A second-order consequence worth naming.
- Third implication
  - The action this quote argues for.

whenToUse.title: Quote + implications
whenToUse.body: When a quotation matters AND you need to spell out the consequences alongside it. Pure quote (no implications) → `quote` slide. Pure list (no quote) → `cards-stack`. split-statement is the hybrid.

whenToUse.title: Expert claims that argue for action
whenToUse.body: Customer voice, analyst quote, founder principle — the kind of phrase that ought to drive a decision. The right panel makes the decision explicit so the audience doesn't have to infer it.

whenToUse.title: Two-line quotation max
whenToUse.body: The left panel is large but not infinite. Past two sentences the italic display font crowds. Trim the quote or split into a `quote` slide followed by an `cards-stack` of implications.

antiPatterns.title: Implications that just paraphrase the quote
antiPatterns.body: If the right-panel bullets are restating what the quote said, the slide is doing two jobs that should be one. Either trust the quote (use `quote`) or make implications that genuinely extend it.

antiPatterns.title: Quote shorter than the implications
antiPatterns.body: When the quote is one phrase and the right panel has three paragraphs of body, the visual balance breaks. Either lengthen the quote (within reason) or move to `split-brief` (lede + findings).

antiPatterns.title: No attribution
antiPatterns.body: The inline-code cite line is what makes a pull quote credible. An unattributed quote in a deck reads as the author's invention; attribution earns the visual weight.

related.name: quote
related.when: the quote IS the slide — no implications needed

related.name: split-brief
related.when: the left carries a thesis statement, not a quote

related.name: split-list
related.when: the right side is supporting points, no quoted claim on the left

components.name: split-steps
components.description: Sequential process — phase label + heading on the left, numbered step timeline on the right with a connecting line.
components.purpose: Use when a multi-step process needs a phase anchor (week N, sprint, milestone) alongside the ordered steps that compose it. Ordered list renders with auto-numbered discs; unordered list uses plain discs.

phase.description: Optional inline-code phase number or label (e.g. '02').

heading.description: Phase heading shown over the watermark phase number.

summary.description: One-sentence summary of what this phase covers.

steps.description: Right-side steps. Use ordered list for numbered discs; lead each with **Title.** then nested body.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: split-steps -->

`02`

## Phase name

One-sentence summary describing what this phase produces.

1. First step
   - What happens and what gets produced.
2. Second step
   - What follows and how it's validated.
3. Third step
   - What closes the phase out.

whenToUse.title: Phase anchor on the left, steps on the right
whenToUse.body: When a phase number, name, and intro paragraph need to read alongside the ordered steps that compose it. The left panel is the orientation; the right panel is the work.

whenToUse.title: Three to five steps per phase
whenToUse.body: Below three the right panel feels under-furnished; above five the timeline spine compresses and the body lines run together. Group adjacent steps or split into two phase slides.

whenToUse.title: Phase chip names the slot
whenToUse.body: The inline-code phase chip (`01`, `02`, `Sprint 3`, `Week 4`) becomes the decorative left-panel watermark. Pick a chip the audience can recognise from a deck cover or roadmap.

antiPatterns.title: Standalone process, no phase anchor
antiPatterns.body: If the slide is one ordered process with no phase context, use `list-steps` or `timeline`. split-steps earns its split only when the left panel adds orientation.

antiPatterns.title: Steps longer than two sentences
antiPatterns.body: Each right-side step is a title plus a sentence or two. If a step needs a paragraph, lift to `list-steps` where the cards have body room.

antiPatterns.title: More than one phase per slide
antiPatterns.body: split-steps is one phase per slide; the watermark and intro paragraph anchor that single phase. Use consecutive split-steps slides for sequential phases.

related.name: list-steps
related.when: standalone ordered process without a phase anchor

related.name: timeline
related.when: horizontal sequence of short-labelled steps

related.name: roadmap
related.when: phased grid across multiple workstreams

related.name: split-list
related.when: left-anchor + right-list shape without a step sequence

related.name: split-brief
related.when: executive framing with substantiating findings rather than steps

components.name: state-chart
components.description: Native state machine diagram — states as a numbered list, transitions as nested inline-code refs.
components.purpose: Use to show a finite-state machine — the discrete states a system can be in and the events that move between them. Authors write a numbered list; each state's index becomes its stable ref so transitions cite numbers, not names. The numbering is the layout: state i renders at row i, so there is no auto-layout problem to solve.

title.description: Slide heading framing the state machine.

eyebrow.description: Optional eyebrow naming the machine or domain.

states.description: One li per state. Index is the stable ref. Trailing inline code is a closed metadata vocabulary: `start`, `end`, or one of the chart-status keywords (on-track, at-risk, blocked, done, live, decision, deferred, warn, pilot, fail). Multiple metadata tokens allowed; order is irrelevant. Unknown trailing codes are left in the rendered label.

transitions.description: Outgoing transitions from a state — one per nested bullet. Each carries a single inline-code arrow `event=>N` or `=>N` (event optional). Target is a state index or the literal `self` for self-loops. Whitespace inside the inline code is insignificant.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: state-chart -->

`Submission lifecycle`

## Document approval flow.

How a draft moves from author to archive.

1. Draft `start`
   - `submit => 2`
   - `discard => 6`
2. Submitted `on-track`
   - `review => 3`
3. In Review
   - `approve => 4`
   - `reject => 1`
   - `revise => self`
4. Approved `done`
   - `publish => 5`
5. Published `live`
   - `archive => 6`
6. Archived `end`

*Rejected drafts return to the author; revisions stay in review.*

whenToUse.title: Finite, named states with discrete events
whenToUse.body: When the slide is about a system with a small set of named places it can be in (Draft / Submitted / Approved / Archived) and the events that move between them (submit, approve, reject). The numbered authoring forces you to enumerate every state up front; the inline refs force you to be explicit about every transition.

whenToUse.title: Sequential authoring as a forcing function
whenToUse.body: Numbering the states makes the author commit to an order. Reading the list top-to-bottom is reading the machine from start to terminal. This is the same forcing function that `list-steps` and `agenda` apply — a sequence the reader can scan in one pass.

whenToUse.title: Native theming without Mermaid overhead
whenToUse.body: Mermaid's `stateDiagram-v2` works but requires a CSS override cascade with `!important` to theme cleanly (see `docs/theming.md`). A native state chart uses palette tokens directly — no overrides, no mmdc subprocess, no version-coupled SVG class names.

antiPatterns.title: More than ~8 states
antiPatterns.body: Vertical stacks of ten or more states stop reading as a machine and start reading as a list. If the system has many states, group them into phases and show one phase at a time, or step back to a higher-level abstraction. The chart's job is to make the topology obvious in one glance.

antiPatterns.title: Hierarchical or parallel states
antiPatterns.body: v1 grammar is one flat list of states with one outgoing arrow per nested bullet. Composite states, orthogonal regions, history nodes — anything Mermaid's `stateDiagram-v2` does and this layout doesn't — belong in a Mermaid fence via the `diagram` component.

antiPatterns.title: Continuous processes
antiPatterns.body: If the diagram is really a workflow with stages that overlap or block (queue depth, throughput, capacity), a `gantt` or `kanban` chart reads better. State charts are for discrete, mutually-exclusive states the system flips between.

related.name: diagram
related.when: the machine has hierarchical states, parallel regions, or guards that need Mermaid's full state-diagram grammar

related.name: journey
related.when: the sequence is a user's path through tasks with mood / affect, not a system's discrete states

related.name: timeline-list
related.when: events are points in time rather than transitions between named states

related.name: list-steps
related.when: a linear procedure with no branching — state-chart is overkill if there are no choices to make

related.name: roadmap
related.when: parallel workstreams across phases, not a single machine's transitions

components.name: stats
components.description: Row of 3–5 stat tiles, each with a big number and a label.
components.purpose: Use for at-a-glance metric rows — quarterly results, headline KPIs. Each tile reads as Big Number + caption.

tiles.description:
One li per stat tile, authored as an ordered list (`1.`). The lead is the number (it renders in display type automatically — no `**…**` needed); the caption is a nested bullet beneath it:

    1. 73%
       - faster close

A bare number with no nested caption won't render as the big number.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: stats -->

`Impact · Pilot Results`

## Six months of results across four product teams.

`Measured against pre-framework baseline, same teams, same market conditions.`

1. 73%
   - faster close
2. 4.2×
   - signal recall
3. $1.2M
   - prevented losses
4. −18d
   - avg cycle time

whenToUse.title: Three to five headline metrics
whenToUse.body: Quarterly results, pilot outcomes, year-end summary — anywhere a small set of independent numbers tells the story together. Each tile reads as a `big-number` in miniature.

whenToUse.title: Numbers are the headline
whenToUse.body: Lead with the number, follow with a one-line caption. The tile is for the metric and a label, not for explanation; if the caption wants a sentence, use `kpi` or `split-metric` instead.

whenToUse.title: Independent metrics, not parts of a whole
whenToUse.body: Stats rows are for headline KPIs that don't sum to anything — close rate, recall rate, dollars saved, days cut. For part-to-whole breakdowns reach for `piechart`.

antiPatterns.title: Six or more tiles
antiPatterns.body: Past five tiles the row compresses and the numbers shrink below boardroom legibility. Split into two rows or move to `kpi` where the dashboard grid gives each metric its own card.

antiPatterns.title: Tiles with no number
antiPatterns.body: If a tile is mostly prose with a small number, the visual hierarchy inverts and the row reads as a list. Stats is for **bold-number + caption** — anything more belongs in `cards-grid`.

antiPatterns.title: Status framing without pills
antiPatterns.body: If each metric needs a target, a trend, and a status indicator, you're authoring a dashboard, not a stats row. Move to `kpi`, which carries that vocabulary.

related.name: big-number
related.when: one number is enough to carry the slide

related.name: kpi
related.when: metrics need targets, trends, and status pills

related.name: split-metric
related.when: one focal KPI with a paragraph of supporting prose

related.name: piechart
related.when: the numbers are parts of a whole, not independent

related.name: progress
related.when: the metrics are completion percentages across workstreams

components.name: statute-stack
components.description: Citation hierarchy — federal / state / local rows with citation, headline obligation, and status.
components.purpose: Use when three or four parallel jurisdictions need to read at a glance: each row carries the jurisdiction label, the citation, the obligation summary, and an effective-date marker.

heading.description: Slide heading framing what the three rows compare.

rows.description: One li per jurisdiction. Lead with the jurisdiction label as a plain text first line; nested ul items carry the citation (inline code), obligation summary, and status (inline code).

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: statute-stack -->

## Jurisdiction comparison framing the three obligations.

- Federal
  - `Citation · Statute name`
  - Headline obligation in one sentence.
  - `Status or effective date`
- State
  - `Citation · Statute name`
  - Headline obligation in one sentence.
  - `Status or effective date`
- Local
  - `Citation · Statute name`
  - Headline obligation in one sentence.
  - `Status or effective date`

whenToUse.title: Three parallel jurisdictions
whenToUse.body: Federal / state / local — or any three peer regimes — that the room must hold side-by-side. The hue rotation cues which row is which without a legend.

whenToUse.title: Citation plus obligation plus status
whenToUse.body: Each row is a three-part record: the citation (mono chip), the headline obligation in prose, the status pill. Use when all three matter; for citation-only, reach for list-tabular.

whenToUse.title: Compliance briefings
whenToUse.body: Reach for statute-stack when the deck reads as a regulatory memo — counsel and operations need the same view of the same record at the same time.

antiPatterns.title: More than four rows
antiPatterns.body: The three-column rail collapses past four jurisdictions. For longer registers move to `lane` (table form) or split across two statute-stack slides by topic.

antiPatterns.title: Citation without obligation
antiPatterns.body: Without the headline obligation sentence, the layout reads as a citation list. Use list-tabular spec when only the citation matters.

antiPatterns.title: Mixed entry shapes
antiPatterns.body: Every row needs the same three parts — citation, obligation, status. A row missing the status pill or with prose instead of a citation breaks the visual contract.

related.name: list-tabular
related.when: the rows are citation-only references, no obligation prose

related.name: obligation-matrix
related.when: obligations cross-tab against actors or controls

related.name: authority-chain
related.when: the rows are a delegation lineage, not parallel jurisdictions

related.name: compare-table
related.when: the comparison is across criteria, not jurisdictions

components.name: timeline-list
components.description: Date-stamped event list — spine with date pills, status pills, and body.
components.purpose: Use for milestone history or annotated timelines. Each item gets a date pill on the left, status pill on the right, body in the middle.

events.description: Ordered list (numbered). One li per event: a leading inline-code date pill, then the title, then an optional trailing inline-code status pill, then nested body bullets — e.g. 1. `2025 Q1` Framework approved `decision`. Status vocabulary: decision / live / at-risk / blocked / done / on-track / deferred.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: timeline-list -->

`Eyebrow · context`

## How it unfolded.

1. `2024 Q3` First milestone
   - One-sentence description of what shipped.
2. `2025 Q1` Second milestone `decision`
   - One-sentence description.
3. `2025 Q3` Third milestone `live`
   - One-sentence description.

whenToUse.title: Milestones in time
whenToUse.body: Project history, regulatory deadlines, deployment phases, incident post-mortems — anywhere the sequence is in calendar time and each entry needs a date, a verdict, and a sentence of body. The date pill anchors the spine.

whenToUse.title: Annotated, not just chronological
whenToUse.body: Reach for timeline-list when each milestone needs a status read (`decision`, `live`, `at-risk`, `done`) AND a sentence of context. For a plain ordered list use `list-steps`; for time-bound bars use `gantt`.

whenToUse.title: Four to seven entries
whenToUse.body: Below four the spine looks empty; past seven the body bullets compress. Trim the long tail or split the timeline by phase — a 'past' deck and a 'next' deck both read better than a twelve-item spine.

antiPatterns.title: Date-less steps
antiPatterns.body: If the items don't carry calendar dates, you have a sequence, not a timeline. Use `list-steps` for an ordered list or `journey` for stage-by-stage progress without a date axis.

antiPatterns.title: Date-range bars
antiPatterns.body: If each milestone needs a start and an end on a shared time axis, the slide is a Gantt chart. Use `gantt` — the bar geometry will convey the durations the pill cannot.

antiPatterns.title: Status pills as decoration
antiPatterns.body: The status pill is a verdict — `decision`, `live`, `at-risk`, `blocked`, `done`. Don't invent freeform tags; the engine only tints the recognised vocabulary, and decorative pills break the at-a-glance read.

related.name: gantt
related.when: milestones occupy date ranges, not single moments

related.name: list-steps
related.when: the sequence has no dates, just an order

related.name: journey
related.when: stage-by-stage progress without calendar dates

related.name: roadmap
related.when: the timeline is forward-looking and bucketed by horizon

related.name: progress
related.when: the events are parallel workstreams with completion percentages

components.name: title
components.description: Opening slide. Dark canvas, centered, no chrome.
components.purpose: First slide of every deck. Sets the topic and the visual tone. Suppresses header, footer, and pagination (or use the universal `silent` modifier for the same effect in one token).

eyebrow.description: Optional category label below the h1 (inline-code paragraph).

subtitle.description: Optional plain-paragraph subtitle below the eyebrow.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: title -->
<!-- _paginate: false -->
<!-- _header: '' -->
<!-- _footer: '' -->

# Deck title goes here

`Category · Date or audience`

One-line subtitle that frames the deck.

whenToUse.title: First slide of every deck
whenToUse.body: Sets topic, audience, and visual tone in one glance. The dark canvas anchors the deck visually so subsequent slides feel like a continuous document.

whenToUse.title: Brand or section bookends
whenToUse.body: Pair with `divider` (mid-deck section breaks) and `closing` (the final slide) for the full anchor trio. All three share the dark-bookend treatment.

whenToUse.title: Pitch and proposal openings
whenToUse.body: When the audience needs the headline and the framing line before any data. The subtitle paragraph is where the framing line goes.

antiPatterns.title: Mid-deck statements
antiPatterns.body: Use `big-number` or `content` for emphatic statements inside a deck. Reaching for the title chrome mid-deck breaks the bookend signal.

antiPatterns.title: Multi-line h1
antiPatterns.body: Keep the h1 to one editorial line. The layout is centered and large — two-line titles get cramped and lose impact.

antiPatterns.title: Header or footer overrides
antiPatterns.body: Don't add back `_header:` or `_footer:` on a title slide. The dark canvas is meant to be uninterrupted; chrome belongs on body slides.

related.name: divider
related.when: mid-deck section breaks — same dark-bookend chrome

related.name: closing
related.when: the final slide — closes the bookend pair

related.name: subtopic
related.when: lighter mid-deck orientation slide on the bright canvas

components.name: verdict-grid
components.description: Options scored against criteria as a verdict matrix.
components.purpose: Use to evaluate 2–4 options against the same set of criteria, with pass/partial/fail badges. Each card represents one option; badges per criterion.

options.description: One outer li per option, lead with **Option name.**. Then one inner li per criterion, each led by a state marker ([x]/[-]/[ ]/[/]) followed by a badge label of AT MOST TWO WORDS. Criteria are shared across every option, in the same order. The last option renders as the focal verdict.

rationale.description: REQUIRED. The final inner li of every option carries NO state marker — one short prose line giving the verdict for that option. This content line is what fills the card; omit it and the card renders empty below the badges.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: verdict-grid -->

## Which option meets the criteria.

- **First option.**
  - [x] First badge
  - [-] Second badge
  - [ ] Third badge
  - One-line rationale giving the verdict for this option.
- **Second option.**
  - [x] First badge
  - [x] Second badge
  - [-] Third badge
  - One-line rationale giving the verdict for this option.
- **Third option.**
  - [x] First badge
  - [x] Second badge
  - [x] Third badge
  - One-line rationale; the last option is the focal verdict. Recommended.

whenToUse.title: Two to four options
whenToUse.body: Each card is one option; the grid keeps two cards per row. Past four options the cards crowd and the criteria badges lose legibility.

whenToUse.title: Shared criteria across cards
whenToUse.body: Every option is scored on the same set of criteria, in the same order. Drifting criteria between cards defeats the at-a-glance scan the layout exists for.

whenToUse.title: Two-word badges
whenToUse.body: Each criterion is a state marker (`[x]` / `[-]` / `[ ]` / `[/]`, shared with `checklist` and `obligation-matrix`) plus a badge label of at most two words — `Residency`, `Self-serve`, `SOC 2`. The badge is chrome that must scan in a glance.

whenToUse.title: A rationale line is required
whenToUse.body: Every option ends with one final inner bullet that carries NO marker — a short prose verdict for that option. It is the body that fills the card, and the last option renders as the focal, recommended verdict.

antiPatterns.title: Exactly two options
antiPatterns.body: Two options with shared criteria belong in `compare-prose` or `split-compare`. verdict-grid earns its layout at 3+ options.

antiPatterns.title: Missing the rationale line
antiPatterns.body: Every option must end with a marker-less prose line — the verdict for that card. Omit it and the card renders empty below the badges, and the focal last card has nothing to recommend. The rationale is required, not optional.

antiPatterns.title: Badge longer than two words
antiPatterns.body: The text after the marker is a badge, not a sentence — two words at most (`Residency`, `Self-serve`). A sentence on a badge line breaks the row scan; prose belongs only on the final rationale line.

antiPatterns.title: Cards with different criteria
antiPatterns.body: When each option needs its own criteria list, the comparison fails — use `cards-stack` so each card has full prose breathing room instead.

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: exactly two options with prose bodies

related.name: split-compare
related.when: two options with a bottom verdict bar

related.name: obligation-matrix
related.when: many regimes scored on shared obligations in a table

related.name: compare-table
related.when: cells are textual values, not state markers

related.name: checklist
related.when: one set of criteria, not many options against them

components.name: word-cloud
components.description: Spiral-packed word cloud — items sized by weight.
components.purpose: Use for qualitative summaries — retrospective themes, survey verbatims. Word size encodes frequency or weight; not a precise data viz.

words.description: One li per word. Format: `word `weight`` where weight is any positive number — a frequency count, a 1–5 rating, a percentage. Words are sized and coloured RELATIVE to each other: the lightest maps to small/muted, the heaviest to the hero size/accent.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: word-cloud -->

## What the team called out this quarter.

- velocity `12`
- ownership `9`
- handoffs `7`
- review `5`

whenToUse.title: Qualitative themes at a glance
whenToUse.body: Retrospective summaries, survey verbatims, theme extraction, sentiment scans. The cloud lands what the corpus is about; the silhouette and the biggest words are the read.

whenToUse.title: Weight is approximate, not exact
whenToUse.body: Word size encodes relative frequency or weight, but the eye reads 'biggest' and 'second biggest' before any precise ratio. Reach for word-cloud when the rank matters more than the count.

whenToUse.title: Eight to twenty items
whenToUse.body: Below eight the spiral looks bare and the layout wastes the canvas. Past about twenty the smallest words become unreadable. Trim the long tail or cap the cloud at a 'top 15' before authoring.

antiPatterns.title: Precise comparisons
antiPatterns.body: If the audience needs to know that 'manifest' is 1.6× 'function', the spiral packing actively misleads. Use `progress` or a bar chart where the eye can compare lengths directly.

antiPatterns.title: Two or three words
antiPatterns.body: A three-word cloud is a list with extra steps. Use `stats` for a metric row or `big-number` for a single weighted headline.

antiPatterns.title: Multi-word phrases
antiPatterns.body: Each li should be a single token. Multi-word phrases blow out the layout and crowd the spiral; if your data is phrases, normalise to keywords first or use `quote` for verbatim text.

related.name: progress
related.when: the weights need precise visual comparison

related.name: stats
related.when: the headline metrics are independent numbers, not a corpus

related.name: piechart
related.when: the items are parts of a whole, not free-form themes

related.name: quote
related.when: the verbatim language matters more than the frequency

related.name: tldr
related.when: the qualitative summary is prose, not a packed cloud
